file_name: SmartRxSystemsInc_20180914_1-A_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_11351705_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_Distributor Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means all proprietary information of Company or a third party disclosed on or after the date hereof by Company to Distributor in any form (written, oral, photographic, electronic, magnetic, or otherwise) which is designated as confidential or proprietary, including, without limitation: technical data; methods; computer programs and related materials; product specifications; documentation; processes; inventions; products under development; sales; marketing; business and financial information; pro formas and projections; customer lists; internal memoranda; and correspondence.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means only information explicitly labeled as such in writing by Company and does not include technical data, methods, computer programs, product specifications, or any customer-related data.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of "Confidential Information" by requiring explicit written labeling and excluding several categories of information traditionally considered confidential, potentially conflicting with trade secret law and creating ambiguity around protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets broadly, including technical or commercial information that has economic value from not being generally known. The modification contradicts this by excluding key types of information like technical data and customer lists from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' which could fail to meet the requirements for trade secret protection under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract language contradicts the premise of trade secret protection by explicitly excluding technical data and customer lists from the definition of "Confidential Information." The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both point out that this exclusion weakens trade secret protection. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful, the exclusion of these types of information undermines the enforceability of the contract under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Company hereby appoints Distributor as Company's exclusive Distributor for the State of Texas, and Distributor accepts the appointment and agrees to represent the Products within the Territory. Company agrees that no other Distributor will be appointed in any other state as a Distributor unless it is either the Company or Distributor, save and except for the state of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: Company hereby appoints Distributor as Company's non-exclusive Distributor for the State of Texas, and Distributor accepts the appointment and agrees to represent the Products within the Territory. Company reserves the right to appoint other distributors in Texas at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text changes the distribution agreement from 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive,' fundamentally altering the distributor's rights and potentially leading to conflicts if other distributors are appointed in the same territory.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct contradiction, changing the agreement to non-exclusive impacts the 'best efforts' obligations possibly implied in the original exclusive agreement. Section 2.306 implies a duty of reasonable diligence and fair dealing in exclusive dealing contracts. By making the distributorship non-exclusive, the company diminishes any implied obligation that it cannot solicit other distributors which could impact sales.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: parties "unless otherwise agreed", the agreement may not only shift the allocation but may also divide the risk or burden. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.304. PRICE PAYABLE IN MONEY, GOODS, REALTY, OR OTHERWISE. (a) The price can be made payable in money or otherwise. If it is payable in whole or in part in goods each party is a seller of the goods which he is to transfer. (b) Even though all or part of the price is payable in an interest in realty the transfer of the goods and the seller's obligations with reference to them are subject to this chapter, but not the transfer of the interest in realty or the transferor's obligations in connection therewith. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.305. OPEN PRICE TERM. (a) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (1) nothing is said as to price; or (2) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (3) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (b) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (c) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (d) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.306. OUTPUT, REQUIREMENTS AND EXCLUSIVE DEALINGS. (a) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (b) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.307. DELIVERY IN SINGLE LOT OR SEVERAL LOTS. Unless otherwise agreed all goods called for by a contract for sale must be tendered in a single delivery and payment is due only on such tender but where the circumstances give either party the right to make or demand delivery in lots the price if it can be apportioned may be demanded for each lot. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.308. ABSENCE OF SPECIFIED PLACE FOR DELIVERY. Unless otherwise agreed (1) the place for delivery of goods is the seller's place of business or if he has none his residence; but (2) in a contract for sale of identified goods which to the knowledge of the parties at the time of contracting are in some other place, that place is the place for their delivery; and (3) documents of title may be delivered through customary banking channels. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.309. ABSENCE OF SPECIFIC TIME PROVISIONS; NOTICE OF TERMINATION. (a) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (b) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite
perturbation.justification: The change from 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive' distributor directly impacts the obligations of the company. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale.' By changing the agreement to non-exclusive, the 'best efforts' clause that would have been imposed on the company is removed, creating a contradiction. The company is no longer obligated to exert best efforts to prevent other distributors from impacting sales, potentially harming the original distributor's sales.

perturbation.original_text: Patents mean all patents and applications relating to the Kiosk or resulting from any Inventions relating to the Smart Pharm Assist Kiosk™.
perturbation.changed_text: Patents mean issued patents only related to the Kiosk and exclude patent applications.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Patents' excludes patent applications, which are legally recognized forms of intellectual property. This could impact the interpretation of rights and obligations related to intellectual property protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S. Code § 154 defines the term 'patent' as referencing a right given with the issuance of a 'patent' by the USPTO. Patent applications, while not patents, confer certain provisional rights that are lost by excluding them from the definition of 'patents'.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 35 / PART II / CHAPTER 14 / § 154 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 35 USC 154 : Contents and term of patent; provisional rights Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 35-PATENTS PART II-PATENTABILITY OF INVENTIONS AND GRANT OF PATENTS CHAPTER 14-ISSUE OF PATENT Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date Regulations §154. Contents and term of patent; provisional rights (a) In General.- (1) Contents .-Every patent shall contain a short title of the invention and a grant to the patentee, his heirs or assigns, of the right to exclude others from making, using, offering for sale, or selling the invention throughout the United States or importing the invention into the Unit
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original contract by excluding patent applications from the definition of "Patents." This creates a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' category because it deviates from legally recognized definitions of intellectual property rights. 35 U.S. Code § 154, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, outlines the "Contents and term of patent; provisional rights," suggesting that patent rights extend beyond solely issued patents. By explicitly excluding patent applications, the modified contract undermines the potential provisional rights associated with pending patents, which are legally recognized and protected to some degree. The original contract included 'applications' implying broader IP coverage and by changing the text to exclude applications, the changed text narrows the scope and could result in disputes about IP protection.
